title: Integer Distance Sets
speaker: Sarah Peluse
abstract: I’ll speak about new joint work with Rachel Greenfeld and Marina Iliopoulou in which we address some classical questions concerning the size and structure of integer distance sets. A subset of the Euclidean plane is said to be an integer distance set if the distance between any pair of points in the set is an integer. Our main result is that any integer distance set in the plane has all but a very small number of points lying on a single line or circle.
tags: Visions in Arithmetic and Beyond: Celebrating Peter Sarnak's Work and Impact

proof_stages.label: Definition and classical hyperbola obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines integer-distance sets and recalls the Anning-Erdős theorem by showing that three noncollinear points force every other point into finitely many intersections of two families of hyperbolas.

proof_stages.label: Line-or-circle concentration as the structural target
proof_stages.summary: Known large finite constructions are concentrated on a line or circle, motivating the theorem that every integer-distance set in an N by N box has all but O((log log N)^C) points on a single line or circle.

proof_stages.label: Extremal consequences of the structure theorem
proof_stages.summary: The structure theorem improves the upper bound for sets with no large collinear or concyclic subsets and, together with an intersection bound for lines and circles, nearly closes the gap in the minimum-diameter problem.

proof_stages.label: Intermediate-degree strategy and lattice normalization
proof_stages.summary: The proof replaces the low-degree hyperbola method by intermediate-degree algebraic geometry, first reducing to a rational lattice model and choosing a parameter k of order log log N.

proof_stages.label: Auxiliary surface encoding of distances
proof_stages.summary: Choosing k points of the set, the proof builds an affine variety X_k whose projective closure is an irreducible surface of degree 2^k and whose rational points of height O(N^2) encode the points of the integer-distance set.

proof_stages.label: Determinant-method covering by low-degree curves
proof_stages.summary: An explicit refinement of the Heath-Brown-Salberger-Walsh determinant method produces a hypersurface not containing X_k but vanishing on all relevant rational points, so after optimizing k the set is covered by few irreducible low-degree curves.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to intersections with individual curves
proof_stages.summary: To finish, it remains to bound the intersection of the integer-distance set with each low-degree curve, separately for curves that are not lines or circles and for lines or circles with many points off them.

proof_stages.label: Auxiliary curve irreducibility and final point count
proof_stages.summary: The argument repeats the construction with carefully chosen auxiliary points and an added curve constraint, proves the resulting high-degree curve irreducible, and then applies curve-level determinant bounds to control each intersection and complete the structure theorem.

examples.name: Collinear integer-spaced points
examples.note: A sequence of points one unit apart on a line makes the definition of an integer-distance set concrete but does not drive the later argument.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker names the obvious construction of integer-spaced points on a line but does not compute or verify anything on that specific example.

passes.reason: It is a lower-dimensional collinear integer-distance set used as the basic example, not to refute a claim.
a collinear integer-distance set is collapsed to a one-dimensional line example rather than a genuinely planar configuration
An equally spaced collinear integer-distance set is a regular one-dimensional collapse of the planar problem, used only as a basic example.
An equally spaced collinear lattice is the lower-dimensional, maximally large/regular infinite integer-distance model rather than a refutation.
A highly regular arithmetic progression collapsed onto one line, used only as the basic example.
A collinear equally spaced set is a one-dimensional collapsed but regular integer-distance construction used as the basic example.
A collinear equally spaced set is a collapsed one-dimensional, highly regular integer-distance set used as a basic example.
An equally spaced one-dimensional integer-distance set in the plane is a collapsed collinear but highly regular example, used only to illustrate the definition.
equally spaced collinear points give a highly regular but one-dimensional collapsed integer-distance set used as a basic example
A collinear equally spaced line-lattice is a highly regular collapsed integer-distance set used only as the basic positive example.
A one-dimensional arithmetic progression in the plane, used as a basic positive example.
a collinear arithmetic progression/lattice is a one-dimensional collapsed but very regular integer-distance set, used as a basic example

examples.name: Anning-Erdős line/circle finite families
examples.note: The finite families with all but one point on a line, and the mentioned circle family, are presented as the motivating evidence for the line-or-circle structure theorem and for conjectured extremizers in the diameter problem.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker explicitly constructs the Anning–Erdős finite family with points [0m(m,0)[0m and [0m(0,y_i)[0m from [0mm^2=x^2-y^2[0m and checks why the distances are integers.

passes.reason: These are systematic finite constructions concentrated entirely or almost entirely on one line or circle, a boundary case of line/circle concentration.
these line- or circle-supported finite families show that no uniform finite bound on noncollinear integer-distance sets can hold
The Anning–Erdős finite families are concentrated on a line or circle and show that no uniform finite bound for noncollinear integer-distance sets can hold.
These arbitrarily large finite non-collinear families are boundary constructions concentrated on one line or circle, ruling out any uniform finite bound after Anning–Erdős.
Systematic finite families concentrated on a line or circle, presented as positive constructions rather than refutations.
These constructions put all or almost all points on a line or circle and show that finite noncollinear integer-distance sets can be arbitrarily large.
These arbitrarily large finite noncollinear families refute any finite bounded analogue of the no-infinite theorem while being concentrated on a line or circle.
These infinite finite-size families, with all but one point on a line or all points on a circle, show that the Anning–Erdős infinite theorem has no bounded finite analogue.
the Anning–Erdős families are positive large finite constructions concentrated on a single line or circle, not sporadic or known optimal objects
These systematic line/concyclic finite families are lower-dimensional special constructions used to show large finite examples exist.
Arbitrarily large finite constructions concentrated on a line or circle, used to show existence rather than refute the stated theorem.
the Anning–Erdős families rule out any absolute finite bound while being maximally concentrated on a line or circle

examples.name: Seven-point set with no forbidden line or circle
examples.note: The computer-found seven-point configuration is used only as a benchmark lower example for the Erdős-type question with no three collinear and no four concyclic points.
examples.engagement_reason: The seven-point example is only cited as the largest known computer-assisted construction, with no properties checked or construction run through.

passes.reason: The seven-point configuration is a known record lower-bound example in general position, but not a proven structural extremum or a refutation.

examples.name: Higher-dimensional orthogonal-plane construction
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker mentions putting compatible planar integer-distance configurations in two orthogonal planes in R^4 to illustrate what is known and what becomes difficult in higher dimensions.
